siteConditions.label: Limited access / steep terrain
siteConditions.description: Steep slopes, dense vegetation, restricted space

objectives.label: Bedrock depth & rippability
objectives.description: Map depth to competent bedrock for foundation design

objectives.label: Utilities & near-surface structures
objectives.description: Map buried pipes, cables, tanks, foundations

objectives.label: Mineral exploration
objectives.description: Delineate ore bodies, structural controls, alteration

objectives.label: Dam foundation investigation
objectives.description: Characterise foundation conditions for dam design

objectives.label: Contamination / plume mapping
objectives.description: Delineate contaminant plumes, leachate, saltwater intrusion

objectives.label: Stratigraphy / layer mapping
objectives.description: Map subsurface layer geometry and thickness

methods.name: Seismic Refraction
limitations: Cannot detect velocity inversions (a low-velocity layer masked beneath a faster layer is invisible)
Requires good ground coupling and a spread of geophones (50-200 m layout)
Cultural vibration (traffic, machinery) degrades first-arrival picks

methods.name: MASW (Multi-channel Analysis of Surface Waves)
limitations: Maximum reliable depth ~30-50 m with active-source MASW
Resolution drops sharply below 1/3 of maximum depth
Passive MASW extends depth but lowers near-surface resolution

methods.name: Electrical Resistivity Tomography (2D ERT)
limitations: Requires good electrode-to-ground contact (difficult on dry gravel, hardpan, or paved surfaces)
Conductive clay overburden suppresses signal from deeper resistive targets
Deep investigations need long layouts (depth ~1/6 of array length)
deliverables: Depth to water table / aquifer geometry

methods.name: Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES)
limitations: 1D point measurement — no lateral continuity between soundings unless densely spaced
Layer equivalence: multiple layer models can fit the same curve
Conductive overburden limits depth of investigation
deliverables: 1D layer resistivity model at each sounding

methods.name: Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)
limitations: Signal is strongly attenuated in conductive (clayey, saline, waterlogged) soils — penetration may fall to <1 m
Maximum reliable depth typically 15-30 m in resistive ground; far less in clay
Antenna frequency trades depth for resolution (low freq = deep but coarse)

methods.name: Magnetic Survey
limitations: Strongly affected by cultural metal (fences, pipelines, reinforced concrete) in urban/industrial areas
Non-unique: many source bodies can produce the same anomaly
Depth estimation from magnetics alone is approximate without modelling

methods.name: Gravity Survey
limitations: Requires very precise station levelling and terrain corrections — slow to acquire and process
Small targets need dense station spacing and excellent elevation control
Near-surface density variations can mask deeper targets

methods.name: Electromagnetic (FDEM)
limitations: Conductive overburden (clay/saline) limits depth of investigation for resistive targets below
Sensitive to cultural metal and power-line interference in urban/industrial areas
Frequency / coil spacing trades depth for resolution; deep targets (>50 m) need low-frequency TDEM instead

methods.name: Geotechnical Drilling (ground truth)
bestFor: SPT & UDS sampling for design parameters
limitations: Point data only — interpolation between boreholes is uncertain without geophysics
High cost per metre and slow mobilisation; access can be difficult on steep or soft ground
Holes must be backfilled / sealed; environmental permitting may apply
deliverables: Index & engineering properties from lab testing
